instruction: Write one complete Java MainActivity file for Android. It should show the text Hello Android using a programmatic TextView. Do not use XML layouts. Do not include unrelated imports.
Fix the Android Java code so it matches the requirement exactly. Return one complete Java file and no extra commentary.
A WebView app fails to load internet pages. Show the minimal AndroidManifest.xml permission needed for internet access and where it goes. Keep the answer short.
output:
Add the INTERNET permission as a direct child of the manifest element, outside the application element:

<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />

    <application>
        ...
    </application>
</manifest>

INTERNET is a normal permission, so it does not need a runtime permission request.

instruction: Write minimal Java Activity code that launches Android's folder picker using ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT_TREE and persists the returned Uri permission in onActivityResult.
Given a persisted tree Uri, write a small Java method using DocumentFile to recursively collect file names under that folder. Avoid java.io.File paths.
Write a minimal Java Activity that creates a WebView programmatically, enables JavaScript, sets a WebViewClient, and loads https://example.com. Include the manifest permission needed.
Explain Android Activity lifecycle to a beginner in under 120 words. Mention onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, and onDestroy.
output: An Activity is a screen. onCreate runs once when the screen is created; set up views and state there. onStart runs when it becomes visible. onResume runs when the user can interact with it. onPause runs when it is partly interrupted, so save quick changes or pause animations. onStop runs when it is no longer visible. onDestroy runs when the Activity is finishing or being removed. Most setup goes in onCreate, active work starts in onResume, and cleanup usually happens in onPause, onStop, or onDestroy.

instruction: Show the minimal AndroidManifest.xml activity entry inside application for .MainActivity with MAIN and LAUNCHER intent-filter.
Convert this Java Activity idea to Kotlin: an Activity creates a TextView in onCreate, sets its text to Hello Android, and uses it as the content view. Do not use XML.
Explain in plain English what minSdk, targetSdk, and compileSdk mean in an Android Gradle project. Keep it concise.
output: minSdk is the oldest Android version your app is allowed to install on. targetSdk tells Android which platform behavior your app is designed for; newer targetSdk values can enable newer behavior rules. compileSdk is the Android SDK version used to compile the app, so it controls which APIs your code can reference at build time. Device support is mainly controlled by minSdk, not compileSdk.
